coredata.dc:title: Research on design, fabrication, and properties of Fe2O3@SiO2/CDs/PEG@nSiO2 nanocomposites
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Hui
Wang, Mengbo
Meng, Chuqian
Tao, Wei
Wang, Chao
Yu, Huilong
coredata.dc:description: Multimodal imaging, which can combine information from different imaging modalities, offers huge advantages. Among various multifunctional images, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) combination with fluorescence imaging has attracted considerable attention for their potential biomedical applications. In this study, we present a facile method for fabrication of core-shell-structured nanocomposite particles. First, Fe2O3 nanoparticles were synthesized as the magnetic core. Then, the Fe2O3 nanoparticles were covered with a silica shell. Carbon dots and PEG could be absorbed into the mesoporous silica layer and then sealed by a dense silica layer (denoted as Fe2O3@SiO2/CDs/PEG@nSiO2). The results show that the as-prepared nanocomposite has a uniform size, strong magnetization and good fluorescence properties, which has application as a bioimaging probe.

Magnetic materials

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis of the Fe2O3@SiO2/CDs/PEG@nSiO2 nanocomposite (a); SEM micrograph of Fe2O3 (b); TEM image of Fe2O3@nSiO2 (c); TEM image of Fe2O3@nSiO2@mSiO2 (d); TEM image of Fe2O3@SiO2/CDs/PEG@nSiO2.
XRD patterns of (a) Fe2O3, (b) Fe2O3@nSiO2@mSiO2, and (c) Fe2O3@SiO2/CDs/PEG@nSiO2 nanoparticles. The standard card of Fe2O3 (JCPDS no. 39-1346) is given as a reference.
(a) The hysteresis loops at 298 K, (b) PL excitation and emission spectra of Fe2O3@SiO2/CDs/PEG@nSiO2 nanoparticle.

head.ce:title: Research on design, fabrication, and properties of Fe2O3@SiO2/CDs/PEG@nSiO2 nanocomposites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials and Energy, University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, Chengdu 610000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Electronic Science and Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Materials, China Academy of Engineering Physics, P. O. Box No. 9-12, Huafengxincun, Jiangyou City, Sichuan Province 621908, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multimodal imaging, which can combine information from different imaging modalities, offers huge advantages. Among various multifunctional images, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) combination with fluorescence imaging has attracted considerable attention for their potential biomedical applications. In this study, we present a facile method for fabrication of core-shell-structured nanocomposite particles. First, Fe2O3 nanoparticles were synthesized as the magnetic core. Then, the Fe2O3 nanoparticles were covered with a silica shell. Carbon dots and PEG could be absorbed into the mesoporous silica layer and then sealed by a dense silica layer (denoted as Fe2O3@SiO2/CDs/PEG@nSiO2). The results show that the as-prepared nanocomposite has a uniform size, strong magnetization and good fluorescence properties, which has application as a bioimaging probe.

ce:section.ce:para: The design and synthesis of multifunctional nanomaterials with magnetic and fluorescence properties has attracted great attention in recent years, owing to their applications in magnetic resonance (MR) and fluorescence imaging [1–3]. Fluorescence imaging affords the best sensitivity and spatial resolution for in vitro imaging, while magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is a non-invasive diagnostic technique which offers an excellent spatial resolution for in vivo imaging. Integration of fluorescence imaging and MR imaging in one system combines the advantages of the two to enhance the quality of bioimaging [4–6].

Fluorescent nanoparticles including quantum dots (QDs) and organic dyes have been widely used for labeling cells and imaging [7,8]. In the past decade, carbon dots (CDs) have been explored for the fabrication of in vitro fluorescence imaging agents, owing to their good biocompatibility, green synthesis, and excellent fluorescence efficiency. Thus, CDs are considered to be the next generation of fluorescent nanomaterials, replacing traditional quantum dots and organic dyes [9]. Among various magnetic nanoparticles, iron oxide particles are promising for in vivo applications owing to their biocompatibility [10]. Great efforts have been devoted to designing and fabricating CDs and iron oxide nanocomposites. Wang et al. attached CDs on the surface of iron oxide using a one-pot solvothermal method [11]. Sahu et al. proposed a method to synthesize Fe3O4@m-SiO2-CD hybrid nanoparticles [12]. Liu et al. synthesized C-Fe3O4 QDs nanoparticles, which has the potential of application in dual MRI imaging [13].

Recently, there have been growing research interests in the design and fabrication of magnetic fluorescence nanocomposite particles with core-shell heterostructures, due to not only to their tailorability and functionality in both the cores and shells, but also to minimize the environmental impact of the cores [14,15]. In this study, a core-shell-structured nanocomposite (Fe2O3@SiO2/CDs/PEG@nSiO2) was fabricated using silica carriers as intermediates, which can be used for realizing magnetic resonance imaging and fluorescence imaging in one system.

The core-shell structure nanocomposites (Fe2O3@SiO2/CDs/PEG@nSiO2) were synthesized according to the strategy shown in Fig. 1 (a). The Fe2O3 nanoparticles were synthesized using a modified solvothermal reaction [16]. The synthesis procedure of Fe2O3@nSiO2@mSiO2 consists of two steps based on a modified Stöber process [17]. CDs were synthesized according to the procedure that was previously reported in the literature [18]. Subsequently, 20 mg of CDs nanoparticles and 0.2 g of PEG (Mw = 2000) were suspended in an absolute acetone solution (40 ml) for 30 min. Then, 0.5 g of Fe2O3@nSiO2@mSiO2 nanoparticle was added to this solution, fully mixing in the fuming hood for 1 h to remove acetone, and thus Fe2O3@nSiO2@mSiO2/CDs/PEG nanoparticles were formed. Finally, a silica layer was further coated on the surface of Fe2O3@nSiO2@mSiO2/CDs/PEG nanoparticles based on the Stöber process, resulting in a final product of Fe2O3@SiO2/CDs/PEG@nSiO2 nanoparticles.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of prepared sample was collected using a Rigaku D/Max-2200 PC X-ray diffractometer with Cu target (40 kV, 40 mA). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained on a field emission JEOL JSM-6700F microscope. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained on a JEM-2100F electron microscope operating at 200 kV. The magnetic properties of the prepared sample were measured by a physical property measurement system (PPMS) (Model PPMS-9, Quantum Design, USA) with applied magnetic fields between −10 and 10 kOe at room temperature. Fluorescent spectra were recorded on an RF-5301PC Shimadzu fluorescent spectroscope.

Fig. 1(b–e) shows b) an SEM images of Fe2O3, and TEM images of c) Fe2O3@nSiO2, d) Fe2O3@nSiO2@mSiO2, and e) Fe2O3@SiO2/CDs/PEG@nSiO2 nanoparticles, respectively. The obtained Fe2O3 nanoparticles have a uniform spherical shape and the average particle size is about 250 nm, as shown in Fig. 1(b). Through a Stöber process using the hydrolysis and condensation of tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) in an ethanol-ammonia mixed solution, a uniform silica layer (∼90 nm in thickness) could be formed on individual Fe2O3 nanoparticle seeds, thus resulting in core-shell Fe2O3@nSiO2 microspheres, as shown in Fig. 1(c). The silica coated magnetic particles are perfectly spherical with a smooth surface and show a clear core-shell structure. A further hydrolysis of TEOS deposition with CTAB on the Fe2O3@nSiO2 spheres led to the formation of uniform Fe2O3@nSiO2@mSiO2 microspheres with a mesoporous silica shell of ∼30 nm in thickness, as shown in Fig. 1(d). Interestingly, the pores are found to be randomly distributed over the siliceous shell, while the middle layer is solid and nonporous. This unique microstructure of the obtained microspheres could be very useful for the adsorption of various guest molecules, such as quantum dots, biomacromolecules, and anticancer drugs. PEG was specifically attached to CDs, which further enhanced the biocompatibility and colloidal stability of the fabricated nanocomposites. After mixing Fe2O3@nSiO2@mSiO2 nanoparticles with a solution containing CDs, CDs could get into the mSiO2 layer through the mesoporous channels of silica. Fig. 1(e) shows the TEM images of Fe2O3@SiO2/CDs/PEG@nSiO2, from which it can be clearly seen that the composite nanoparticles still retain the core-shell structure with Fe2O3 as the core, and the compact layer is about 140 nm in thickness. Careful observation shows that the CDs are deposited into the layer, as shown in Fig. 1(e).

The XRD pattern of the as-obtained Fe2O3, Fe2O3@nSiO2@mSiO2, and Fe2O3@SiO2/CDs/PEG@nSiO2 nanoparticles are shown in Fig. 2 . All the diffraction peaks can be ascribed to a pure cubic phase of Fe2O3 (JCPDS no. 39-1346). No second phase is observed in the XRD pattern, indicating SiO2 is an amorphous phase.

Magnetic characterization using a magnetometer at 298 K reveals that the Fe2O3, Fe2O3@nSiO2@mSiO2, and Fe2O3@SiO2/CDs/PEG@nSiO2 spheres have magnetization saturation values of 66, 49, and 35 emu/g (Fig. 3 (a)), respectively. Additionally, no hysteresis was observed on the magnetization curves for all samples, indicating the superparamagnetic characteristic of these materials. The prepared Fe2O3@SiO2/CDs/PEG@nSiO2 spheres have excellent magnetic responsivity and redispersibility, which is an advantage for their applications. The CDS and Fe2O3@SiO2/CDs/PEG@nSiO2 were investigated by PL spectroscopy at room temperature. The photoluminescence excitation spectrum of CDs consists of two main peaks at 401 nm and 521 nm under UV excitation of 365 nm (inset in Fig. 3(b)). For Fe2O3@SiO2/CDs/PEG@nSiO2 nanoparticles, a strong PL emission peak is centered at 523 nm and strong blue PL emission is observed when it is excited at a wavelength of 365 nm. The excitation spectrum monitored at 523 nm shows a wide peak with a maximum value of 405 nm. These results suggest that the PL of the original CDs dispersions was retained in the luminescent magnetic composites. CDs could enter the mSiO2 layer. This core-shell nanocomposite combines magnetism and fluorescence, which has potential application as a bioimaging probe.

In this study, multifunctional nanoparticles with a superparamagnetic Fe2O3 core and silica shell was designed and synthesized. Carbon dots and PEG could be absorbed into the mesoporous silica layer and then sealed by a dense silica layer. This as-prepared nanocomposite has a uniform size, strong magnetization, and good fluorescence properties. Based on the above features, the Fe2O3@SiO2/CDs/PEG@nSiO2 nanoparticle could be exploited for multimodal FL/MRI dual-modal bioimaging. Furthermore, PEG can be absorbed into the mesoporous silica layer, which may provide a new way to design and synthesis novel dual-modality bioimaging and drug delivery nanoparticles.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 51575501 and 51402045), China Scholarship Council (Grant No. 2016060775044), Major Project of Education Department in Sichuan (Grant No. 18ZB0229), Sichuan Province Science and Technology Support Program (Grant No. 18ZDYF2973), Chinese Universities Scientific Fund (Grant No. 2672018ZYGX2018J084) and the CAEP Foundation, China (No. 2015B0203030).
